core_meaning: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる means 'to show' — to deliberately cause someone else to see or look at something. Unlike just seeing something by chance, <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる implies an intentional act of presenting or displaying something to an audience.

sentences.jp: <ruby>新<rt>あたら</rt></ruby>しいスマホ、<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せてよ！
sentences.notes: The て-form + よ makes this a casual, direct request between friends — a very natural everyday use of <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる.

sentences.jp: <ruby>先生<rt>せんせい</rt></ruby>に<ruby>宿題<rt>しゅくだい</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せた。
sentences.translation: I showed my homework to the teacher.
sentences.notes: The basic [<ruby>人<rt>ひと</rt></ruby>に<ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる] pattern: someone is the recipient (に) and something is the object (を).

sentences.jp: パスポートをご<ruby>提示<rt>ていじ</rt></ruby>いただく<ruby>前<rt>まえ</rt></ruby>に、こちらをご<ruby>覧<rt>らん</rt></ruby>ください — あ、いや、パスポートを<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せていただけますか。
sentences.translation: Could you please show me your passport?
sentences.notes: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せていただけますか is a polite request form using the て-form + いただけますか, appropriate for customer service or official situations.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>はステージで<ruby>素晴<rt>すばら</rt></ruby>しいパフォーマンスを<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せた。
sentences.translation: She gave a wonderful performance on stage.
sentences.notes: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる can mean 'to put on a display of' or 'to demonstrate' — here it captures the nuance of showing one's skill to an audience.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼<rt>かれ</rt></ruby>は<ruby>弱<rt>よわ</rt></ruby>いところを<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せたくないので、いつも<ruby>強<rt>つよ</rt></ruby>がっている。
sentences.translation: He doesn't want to show his weak side, so he always acts tough.
sentences.notes: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる here means 'to reveal' or 'to expose' an aspect of oneself — it extends beyond physical objects to personal qualities or emotions.

dont_use: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる requires an intentional agent doing the showing; don't use it when something is simply visible or you happen to see it — use <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える (to be visible) instead. Don't confuse the structure: it's [<ruby>人<rt>ひと</rt></ruby>に<ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる], so be careful not to swap に and を. Also avoid using <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる for 'watching' a movie or show — that's <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る.

confused_with.contrast: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える means something is spontaneously visible or appears a certain way — there is no deliberate agent. For example, <ruby>山<rt>やま</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える means 'the mountain can be seen (from here),' not that anyone is showing it. <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる, by contrast, always involves someone intentionally presenting something to be looked at.

frequency: Very common in daily speech across all registers. <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せて is one of the most frequent casual request forms learners encounter early on. Also appears frequently in written and formal contexts.
anki_hint: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる = intentional showing [<ruby>人<rt>ひと</rt></ruby>に<ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる]; if nobody is choosing to show it, use <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える instead.
